unit.subject: English
unit.unitTitle: The Digital World and Future Innovation
unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 9: The Digital World and Future Innovation — the final unit of Year 7! In this unit you will explore how digital technology, robotics, and innovative farming are reshaping daily life across East Africa and the wider world. You will read about young people who use smartphones, coding skills, and creative thinking to solve real problems in their communities. Through reading, writing, speaking, and grammar activities, you will sharpen your English skills while thinking critically about the role of technology in our lives.
unit.learningPath:
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn a few words each day and add them to your Word Wall.
Work through the seven lessons in the Grammar file (Present Perfect + Just, Connectives, Either/Neither/Both, Past Continuous, Complex Noun Phrases, Adjective + Infinitive, Verb + -ing).
Read the Story file, “Shadows in Mogadishu”, and answer the comprehension questions.
Come back to this lesson file and work through all nine sections in order.
Finish with the Check Your Learning self-assessment at the end.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To make someone feel worried or upset.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The loud notification sound began to agitate the students during the coding test.
practiceSentences: The loud notification sound began to agitate the students during the coding test.
Constant breaking-news alerts can agitate readers, leaving them anxious long after the screen goes dark.
Why does the flashing update icon agitate some users while others barely notice it?
'Do not agitate the testers with last-minute changes,' warned Samira, 'or the demo will collapse.'
Endless comparison online used to agitate me, so I now silence notifications after eight o'clock.

dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'agitate', use it in a sentence, and connect it to The Digital World and Future Innovation.

readings.title: The Digital World and Future Innovation: source text 1
readings.theme: The Digital World and Future Innovation
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Welcome to Unit 9: The Digital World and Future Innovation — the final unit of Year 7! In this unit you will explore how digital technology, robotics, and innovative farming are reshaping daily life across East Africa and the wider world. You will read about young people who use smartphones, coding skills, and creative thinking to solve real problems in their communities. Through reading, writing, speaking, and grammar activities, you will sharpen your English skills while thinking critically about the role of technology in our lives.
This unit brings together everything you have learned this year. You will use reported speech to relay what others have said about technology, conditionals (if/unless) to discuss possible outcomes, and complex noun phrases to describe inventions precisely. You will also practise debating, diary writing, and report writing — all essential skills for Cambridge Lower Secondary English.
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Read and understand a passage about digital innovation and discuss its ideas confidently in English.
Use reported speech to relay what someone has said: “She said that the app had just been updated.”
Form conditional sentences with if and unless: “If we use technology wisely, it will help us.”
Build complex noun phrases to describe tech inventions precisely: “a solar-powered automated irrigation system.”
Write a structured debate argument, a digital detox diary, or a future farming report.
Present your ideas about technology clearly in a spoken discussion or debate.
Use at least fifteen new vocabulary words from this unit confidently in speech and writing.
Think critically about technology addiction, misinformation, and sustainability.
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn a few words each day and add them to your Word Wall.
Work through the seven lessons in the Grammar file (Present Perfect + Just, Connectives, Either/Neither/Both, Past Continuous, Complex Noun Phrases, Adjective + Infinitive, Verb + -ing).
Read the Story file, “Shadows in Mogadishu”, and answer the comprehension questions.
Come back to this lesson file and work through all nine sections in order.
Finish with the Check Your Learning self-assessment at the end.
Before you read the passage below, make sure you know these important words. They appear throughout the unit. If any word is new, look it up in your Vocabulary file or ask your AI tutor to explain it.
conceive — to think of or create an idea or plan in your mind.
acquire — to get or gain something, usually by effort or learning.
introduce — to make something known for the first time; to bring something new.
reveal — to show or make something known that was hidden or secret.
determine — to find out or decide something after thinking about it.
versatile — able to do many different things or adapt to many situations.
rudimentary — basic or simple; not fully developed.
agitate — to make someone feel worried or upset.
ostracise — to exclude someone from a group or society.
bamboozle — to trick or confuse someone.
demeanor — the way a person behaves or shows their feelings.
eccentric — strange or unusual in a way that is interesting or different.
blunt — speaking in a direct, honest way without trying to be polite.
fugitive — a person who is running away or trying to escape.

readings.title: The Digital World and Future Innovation: source text 2
readings.theme: The Digital World and Future Innovation
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
compare — to look at two or more things to see how they are similar or different.
converge — to come together from different directions to meet at one point.
obscure — not well known or hard to understand.
omit — to leave out or not include something.
adequate — good enough for what is needed; sufficient.
lapse — a small mistake or failure that happens because you forget something.
intrigue — a secret plan or a mysterious event that makes people curious.
Reading: Smart Farms and Digital Dreams
Read this passage carefully. Pay attention to the bold vocabulary words. After reading, you will answer comprehension questions and discuss the ideas.
In recent years, digital technology has begun to reveal new possibilities for communities across East Africa. From Hargeisa to Nairobi, young innovators are finding versatile ways to use smartphones, sensors, and coding to solve problems that once seemed impossible. One of the most exciting areas of change is future farming — using technology to grow more food with fewer resources.
In a small town outside Mogadishu, a group of secondary-school students conceived a plan to help local farmers. They had noticed that many families were struggling because rainfall had become unpredictable. Crops failed, and food prices grew worse every season. The students decided to acquire basic coding skills and build a simple weather-monitoring app. Their teacher, Mr. Noah, introduced them to rudimentary electronics — small sensors that could measure temperature, humidity, and soil moisture.
At first, some adults were sceptical. A local shopkeeper said that students were too young to build anything useful. He told them that technology would not replace hard work. But the students were not agitated by the criticism. They determined that if they worked together, they could prove the doubters wrong. Unless they tried, they would never know what was possible.
Within three months, the team had built a working prototype. The app collected data from small sensors placed in the soil and sent alerts to farmers’ phones. If the soil was too dry, the app told the farmer to water the crops. Unless the temperature dropped below a safe level, the system stayed quiet. One farmer, Halimo, reported that she had just saved an entire field of maize thanks to an early warning from the app.
Word about the project spread quickly. People from neighbouring villages converged on the school to see demonstrations. A journalist from a Mogadishu radio station interviewed the students. She asked them how they had learned to code. Samira, the team leader, replied that they had taught themselves using free online tutorials. She said that anyone with a phone and internet access could learn the same skills.
The project also introduced a drone that could photograph fields from above. By comparing images taken at different times, the students could spot which parts of a field were healthy and which were struggling. This kind of analysis, once obscure and expensive, was now adequate for a school team with basic equipment.
Mr. Noah told a visiting teacher that the students had just completed their most ambitious test. He reported that if the government supported similar programmes, thousands of farms could benefit. He said that unless young people were given opportunities to innovate, the country would fall behind.

readings.title: Shadows in Mogadishu, part 1
readings.theme: The Digital World and Future Innovation
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
The compound walls were cream-coloured, edged with modern vines. From the rooftop of Selma's apartment building, the skyline of Mogadishu looked sharp and full of life — construction cranes beside old domes, taxis jostling through roundabouts, teenagers laughing as they left tutoring centres.
Samira leaned astride the bench's armrest, sketchpad on her lap. She had just finished a rough drawing of the sunset when her younger brother barged in.
"Did you hear about the boy from Hamar Weyne?" Noah asked.
"No," Selma replied, barely looking up.
"He was ostracised from school. They say he was caught hacking the computer lab!"
Samira blinked. "Hacking? Like, code or stealing?"
"Both, maybe. No one knows. It's all over X (the app formerly known as Twitter)."
She sighed. Mogadishu buzzed with digital gossip. But Selma, age fifteen, preferred fact over fiction.
At Ocean Breeze International School, news spread faster than light. The next morning, Selma overheard classmates comparing the boy's story.
"I heard he's a fugitive, hiding somewhere near KM4."
"No, no," said another, "he's in custody. His uncle revealed it on Facebook Live."
"People just want to bamboozle each other with drama," Selma muttered.
During lunch, she sat with her best friend, Amal, who was versatile in everything from coding to poetry.
"We don't even know the guy," Amal said. "Why is everyone so quick to judge?"
"I want to know what really happened," Selma said. "Not what TikTok says."
Later, she spoke with her tech club mentor, Mr. Adam, a patient, blunt man who ran a small robotics lab on the school's second floor.
"You want the real story?" he said. "Then do what smart people do — acquire real information."
"Start asking. But gently. With care."
Samira began by reaching out to Samira, a quiet girl who had been close to the accused student.
"His name's Sami," Samira said. "He's... different. Eccentric, maybe. Always coding, building circuits from old radios. But not dangerous."
"There was a lapse in the exam server last week. People panicked. Teachers needed someone to blame."
Samira listened, her mind forming connections.
"What was his demeanour like that day?" she asked.
Sami's silence had become a blank canvas for gossip. Some said he had confessed. Others claimed he had vanished.
That evening, Selma couldn't stop thinking. Her mind was conceiving a plan: an article for the school blog.
She spent the night comparing rumours, tracing message threads, and speaking with classmates who had known Sami.
She even visited the dusty IT server room the next morning, asking for archived network logs. The technician, surprised but curious, let her observe. There, among the logs, was a timestamp proving Sami had emailed a teacher minutes before the system crash. The content described a wireless experiment — not a hack.
By Friday, she had just completed a rough draft: "Misjudged in the Digital Age."
In it, she presented evidence Sami had been working on a separate wireless project — one that accidentally overloaded the lab's router. He wasn't a hacker. Just a curious kid caught in a tech lapse.
She explained how bias grows in silence. She used phrases like "a lull in truth" and "an obscure, misunderstood project." Her language was careful — not to excuse, but to question.

readings.title: Shadows in Mogadishu, part 2
readings.theme: The Digital World and Future Innovation
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
The blog post was uploaded Saturday morning. It caused instant discussion.
In the school library, students were converging at the computers.
"Yeah. I didn't know Sami had actually shared his plans with the computer teacher before!"
"Neither did I. The post says the logs prove it."
Sami hadn't been a hacker. He had been misunderstood. Even the head of IT commented, saying the article helped ease tension.
By Monday, Sami's name was cleared. He was exonerated by school officials and invited back.
Still, the damage had changed him. Selma saw him during robotics club — quieter, more cautious.
He nodded. "It's weird. Like walking through fog."
"I'm glad you're here. I almost didn't write that article," Selma confessed.
"It helped," Sami replied. "Not just me. People are listening more carefully now."
Later, Mr. Adam congratulated Selma. "It's not easy to speak against the crowd. It takes guts."
"It was difficult to determine what to omit," Selma said. "Too much, and I'd agitate people. Too little, and I'd hide the point."
He nodded. "But you chose well. That's journalism."
Weeks later, Selma was selected to present at the Somali Young Writers' Forum. She prepared a multimedia talk titled "Perception vs. Reality: A Student's Dilemma."
During her presentation, she used a rudimentary animation to show how rumours spread. She included screenshots, student quotes, and even a short comparison between how Sami had been treated versus another student who had admitted to plagiarism months earlier.
After her talk, a boy from another school approached her.
"I've just started a youth podcast. Would you consider guesting?"
Samira smiled. "Let me check with my parents."
In the following weeks, her episode aired. She spoke about the need to recognise misinformation and to give people space to explain.
One listener messaged: "Your story helped me reconnect with a cousin I'd ostracised. I realised I never asked him for his side."
And another wrote, "My sister was wrongly blamed at school last year. After hearing you, I realised how deeply that affected her."
For Selma, it wasn't fame she wanted. It was clarity.
And in the city she called home, where internet cables crossed old alleyways and stories raced through smart devices, clarity was sometimes the rarest thing of all.
A month later, Selma found herself sitting on that same rooftop bench, sketchpad in hand. The wind carried sounds of evening adhaan, and her brother Noah was back from school, this time holding a drone kit their uncle had mailed from Garowe.
"Can you help me build this?" he asked.
She looked at the box, then at her sketchbook, and smiled.
"Let's build it — and write about it afterward. But first, we do it right. Step by step. No shortcuts."
In that moment, Selma understood something simple but powerful: clarity doesn't always come in a rush. Sometimes it comes slowly, piece by piece — just like a story, or a life.

grammar.title: Present Perfect + Just
grammar.explanation:
What it means and why it matters
The present perfect tense is used to talk about actions that have happened at an unspecified time before now. When we add just, it means the action happened very recently. This is important in the digital world because technology changes fast — we often need to share updates and news that feel immediate and fresh. For example, if you say, “She has just uploaded the code,” it tells us the upload happened only a moment ago.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What it means and why it matters
The present perfect tense is used to talk about actions that have happened at an unspecified time before now. When we add just, it means the action happened very recently. This is important in the digital world because technology changes fast — we often need to share updates and news that feel immediate and fresh. For example, if you say, “She has just uploaded the code,” it tells us the upload happened only a moment ago.
How to form it
Follow these steps:
Identify the subject: the person or thing doing the action (e.g., She, They, I).
Use has or have: has for singular (he, she, it) and have for plural (they, we) and with I and you.
Add the past participle: the third form of the verb (e.g., uploaded, finished, built).
Place just before the past participle: to show the action happened recently.
Structure:
Singular: Subject + has + just + past participle — “She has just uploaded the code.”
Plural: Subject + have + just + past participle — “They have just finished the app.”
Variations and exceptions
Just always goes before the past participle, not after it.
We do not use just with specific time references like yesterday or last week. Say “She has just finished” (not “She has just finished yesterday”).
In informal speech, some people use the past simple instead: “She just finished.” In academic and formal writing, the present perfect form is preferred.
Common mistakes
Mistake: “She has uploaded just the app.” ✗  (just in the wrong place)
Fix: “She has just uploaded the app.” ✓
Mistake: “She just recognised the error.” ✗  (missing has/have)
Fix: “She has just recognised the error.” ✓
Memory tip
Think of just as a tiny time marker that means “moments ago.” Remember: “Just happened, just in time!” The event is fresh like a notification that has just appeared on your phone screen.
Practice 1
Complete each sentence using the present perfect + just:
They ______ (just / finish) building the prototype.
I ______ (just / download) the new coding software.
He ______ (just / arrive) at the robotics workshop.
We ______ (just / receive) the sensor data from the farm.
She ______ (just / complete) her digital detox diary.
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply present perfect + just in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Connectives
grammar.explanation:
What it means and why it matters
Connectives are words that link ideas together. They help us explain reasons, show results, make contrasts, and express purposes. When we discuss technology, connectives help us build clear, logical arguments — for example, explaining why an app works or what happens when we use too much screen time.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What it means and why it matters
Connectives are words that link ideas together. They help us explain reasons, show results, make contrasts, and express purposes. When we discuss technology, connectives help us build clear, logical arguments — for example, explaining why an app works or what happens when we use too much screen time.
Key connectives for this unit:
because — to give a reason.
so that — to show purpose.
although — to show contrast.
therefore — to show a result.
How to use them
because: “The farmers use the app because it sends weather alerts.”
so that: “She coded the programme so that it would run on low-cost phones.”
although: “Although the internet was slow, the students managed to submit their project.”
therefore: “The sensor detected dry soil; therefore, the app sent a watering alert.”
Variations
Connectives can start a sentence: “Although it was a rudimentary prototype, it worked well.”
So that can be used with future tense: “I will practise coding every day so that I improve.”
Common mistakes
Mistake: “The server crashed the students lost their data.” ✗  (missing connective)
Fix: “The server crashed; therefore, the students lost their data.” ✓
Do not swap because and so that. Use because for a reason, so that for a purpose.
Memory tip
Think: “Connectives Connect!” They are the glue that holds your digital-world arguments together.
Practice 2
Fill in the blank with because, so that, although, or therefore:
I backed up my files ______ I would not lose them.
______ the battery was low, the drone still completed its scan.
She shared her code online ______ other students could learn from it.
The app crashed ______ there was a bug in the latest update.
The internet was slow; ______, the download took much longer than expected.
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply connectives in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Either / Neither / Both
grammar.explanation:
What it means and why it matters
Understanding how to use either, neither, and both helps you express choices, agreements, and disagreements clearly. In the tech world, you often need to compare two options — two apps, two devices, or two solutions — and these words help you do that precisely.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What it means and why it matters
Understanding how to use either, neither, and both helps you express choices, agreements, and disagreements clearly. In the tech world, you often need to compare two options — two apps, two devices, or two solutions — and these words help you do that precisely.
How to use them
Either — one of two options: “You can use either the laptop or the tablet.”
Neither — not one and not the other: “Neither app is working properly.”
Both — the two together: “Both apps have been updated.”
Important grammar rules
Either takes a singular noun and verb: “Either device is suitable.”
Neither also takes a singular verb: “Neither option is available.”
Both takes a plural noun and verb: “Both systems are connected.”
Common mistakes
Mistake: “Neither options are good.” ✗  (plural verb with neither)
Fix: “Neither option is good.” ✓
Mistake: “Both app are updated.” ✗  (singular noun with both)
Fix: “Both apps are updated.” ✓
Memory tip
Either = Election (choose one). Neither = Not one (none). Both = Both together (two).
Practice 3
Fill in with either, neither, or both:
You can connect to ______ the Wi-Fi or the mobile data. (one of two)
______ of the chargers is working. We need a new one. (not one, not the other)
______ students passed the coding test. Well done! (the two together)
I have tried ______ browsers, but ______ loads the page correctly. (two / not one)

grammar.title: Past Continuous
grammar.explanation:
What it means and why it matters
The past continuous describes actions that were happening at a specific moment in the past. It helps us set the scene in stories and describe background actions. For example: “While the students were coding, the teacher was checking the server.” It shows two actions happening at the same time.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What it means and why it matters
The past continuous describes actions that were happening at a specific moment in the past. It helps us set the scene in stories and describe background actions. For example: “While the students were coding, the teacher was checking the server.” It shows two actions happening at the same time.
How to form it
Subject + was/were + verb(-ing)
I / he / she / it → was: “She was testing the app.”
You / we / they → were: “They were building the drone.”
Negative: add not → “They were not (weren’t) paying attention to the screen.”
Question: swap subject and was/were → “Were they coding all afternoon?”
Common mistakes
Mistake: “They was building the drone.” ✗  (wrong form of to be)
Fix: “They were building the drone.” ✓
Mistake: “She was test the app.” ✗  (missing -ing)
Fix: “She was testing the app.” ✓
Memory tip
Think of the past continuous as a “video playing in the background.” The action was ongoing, like a live stream you can picture in your mind.
Practice 4
Complete each sentence using the past continuous:
Yesterday at 4 PM, I ______ (research) future farming techniques.
While she ______ (present) her project, the Wi-Fi disconnected.
They ______ (not listen) when the teacher explained the coding task.
______ he ______ (use) the drone when it ran out of battery?
The students ______ (converge) in the lab when the alarm sounded.

grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply past continuous in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Complex Noun Phrases
grammar.explanation:
What it means and why it matters
A complex noun phrase is a group of words that work together to describe a person, place, thing, or idea in more detail. In the digital world, we use complex noun phrases to describe inventions, apps, and devices precisely. Instead of saying “a system,” we can say “a solar-powered, automated irrigation system,” which gives a much clearer picture.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What it means and why it matters
A complex noun phrase is a group of words that work together to describe a person, place, thing, or idea in more detail. In the digital world, we use complex noun phrases to describe inventions, apps, and devices precisely. Instead of saying “a system,” we can say “a solar-powered, automated irrigation system,” which gives a much clearer picture.
How to build one
Start with the noun: the main word you want to describe (e.g., system, device, app).
Add adjectives before it: opinion, size, age, colour, material (e.g., “a small, lightweight sensor”).
Use compound modifiers (hyphenated): “a solar-powered charger”, “a user-friendly platform”.
Add phrases after the noun: prepositional phrases or relative clauses (e.g., “the app that monitors soil moisture”).
Examples from this unit:
“an obscure but intriguing wireless experiment” — two adjectives + noun.
“a rudimentary, student-built weather-monitoring application” — adjective + compound modifier + compound noun.
“the high-resolution agricultural drone that photographed the fields” — adjective + adjective + noun + relative clause.
Common mistakes
Mistake: forgetting hyphens in compound modifiers: “a solar powered charger.” ✗
Fix: “a solar-powered charger.” ✓
Mistake: putting too many adjectives without commas: “a small lightweight waterproof sensor.” ✗
Fix: “a small, lightweight, waterproof sensor.” ✓
Memory tip
Think of complex noun phrases as “super nouns.” The noun is the core, and modifiers are the upgrades — like adding features to an app. The more detail you add, the clearer your description becomes!
Practice 5
Build a complex noun phrase from the parts given, then use it in a sentence:
Noun: drone. Modifiers: small, solar-powered, lightweight.
Noun: platform. Modifiers: free, open-source, multilingual, coding.
Noun: sensor. Modifiers: the, waterproof, that measures soil moisture.

grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.

grammar.title: Adjective + Infinitive
grammar.explanation:
What it means and why it matters
The adjective + infinitive structure uses an adjective followed by to + a base verb. We use it to express feelings, opinions, and states of being. In the digital world, this structure helps us describe reactions to technology: “It is difficult to determine the truth online” or “She was eager to acquire new coding skills.”
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What it means and why it matters
The adjective + infinitive structure uses an adjective followed by to + a base verb. We use it to express feelings, opinions, and states of being. In the digital world, this structure helps us describe reactions to technology: “It is difficult to determine the truth online” or “She was eager to acquire new coding skills.”
How to form it
Choose an adjective: easy, difficult, happy, eager, important, safe, dangerous, exciting.
Add to: after the adjective.
Use the infinitive (base verb): the plain form of the verb.
Examples:
“It is difficult to omit important data from a report.”
“She was eager to acquire the latest coding skills.”
“It is important to recognise misinformation online.”
“The app is easy to use even for beginners.”
Common mistakes
Mistake: “It is difficult omitting a key detail.” ✗  (gerund instead of infinitive)
Fix: “It is difficult to omit a key detail.” ✓
Mistake: “She was eager acquire new skills.” ✗  (missing to)
Fix: “She was eager to acquire new skills.” ✓
Memory tip
Think: “Adjectives love to describe!” The adjective tells us how we feel, and the infinitive tells us about what.
Practice 6
Complete each sentence with the correct adjective + infinitive:
It is ______ (important) ______ (check) your sources before sharing news online.
The students were ______ (excited) ______ (present) their app at the science fair.
It is ______ (dangerous) ______ (share) personal information on social media.
He was ______ (happy) ______ (reveal) the results of his experiment.

writing.title: Writing 1:
writing.promptAndInstructions:
In this section, you will choose one of three writing projects. Each project uses the vocabulary, grammar, and ideas from this unit. The goal is to practise extended writing while thinking critically about the digital world.
Option A: Debate — Is Technology Helping or Harming Young People?
Write a structured debate argument (about 250 words) arguing for or against this statement: “Technology is doing more harm than good to young people.”
Your argument should include:
An introduction stating your position clearly.
Three main points with evidence or examples. Use conditionals: “If young people spend too much time on screens, they will...”
A counter-argument acknowledging the other side: “Some people say that... However,...” Use reported speech here.
A conclusion summarising your view.
Example opening: “I believe that technology is helping young people more than it is harming them. If students have access to online learning platforms, they will be able to study subjects that their schools do not offer.”
Option B: Digital Detox Diary
Imagine you spend one full week without any digital devices — no phone, no tablet, no computer, no television. Write a diary covering three days of that week (about 250 words total). For each day, describe:
What you did instead of using technology.

quizzes.quizTitle: The Digital World and Future Innovation checkpoint
quizzes.question: What does 'agitate' mean?
quizzes.options: To come together from different directions to meet at one point. | To make someone feel worried or upset. | To make something known for the first time; to bring something new. | To show or make something known that was hidden or secret.
quizzes.correctAnswer: To make someone feel worried or upset.
quizzes.explanation: The loud notification sound began to agitate the students during the coding test.

quizzes.quizTitle: The Digital World and Future Innovation checkpoint
quizzes.question: What does 'conceive' mean?
quizzes.options: To make something known for the first time; to bring something new. | To see or hear something and know what it is; to identify. | To show or make something known that was hidden or secret. | To think of or create an idea or plan in your mind.
quizzes.correctAnswer: To think of or create an idea or plan in your mind.
quizzes.explanation: The team conceived a clever plan to build a weather-monitoring app.

quizzes.quizTitle: The Digital World and Future Innovation checkpoint
quizzes.question: What does 'converge' mean?
quizzes.options: To come together from different directions to meet at one point. | To get or gain something, usually by effort or learning. | To see or hear something and know what it is; to identify. | To show or make something known that was hidden or secret.
quizzes.correctAnswer: To come together from different directions to meet at one point.
quizzes.explanation: Students from three schools converged at the robotics competition.

quizzes.quizTitle: The Digital World and Future Innovation checkpoint
quizzes.question: What does 'introduce' mean?
quizzes.options: To find out or decide something after thinking about it. | To get or gain something, usually by effort or learning. | To make something known for the first time; to bring something new. | To see or hear something and know what it is; to identify.
quizzes.correctAnswer: To make something known for the first time; to bring something new.
quizzes.explanation: The teacher introduced us to a new coding language this term.

quizzes.quizTitle: The Digital World and Future Innovation checkpoint
quizzes.question: What does 'reveal' mean?
quizzes.options: Speaking in a direct, honest way without trying to be polite; also: not sharp. | To find out or decide something after thinking about it. | To get or gain something, usually by effort or learning. | To show or make something known that was hidden or secret.
quizzes.correctAnswer: To show or make something known that was hidden or secret.
quizzes.explanation: The data analysis revealed that the sensor was sending incorrect readings.

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: To make someone feel worried or upset. The loud notification sound began to agitate the students during the coding test.
To think of or create an idea or plan in your mind. The team conceived a clever plan to build a weather-monitoring app.
To come together from different directions to meet at one point. Students from three schools converged at the robotics competition.
To make something known for the first time; to bring something new. The teacher introduced us to a new coding language this term.
To show or make something known that was hidden or secret. The data analysis revealed that the sensor was sending incorrect readings.
To see or hear something and know what it is; to identify. I can recognise fake news because the sources are never named.
To get or gain something, usually by effort or learning. She worked hard to acquire the coding skills she needed for the project.
To find out or decide something after thinking about it. We need to determine which app is the most reliable before recommending it.
Speaking in a direct, honest way without trying to be polite; also: not sharp. Mr. Adam gave blunt advice: 'Stop guessing and start researching.'
Strange or unusual in a way that is interesting or different. The eccentric inventor built a robot from old radio parts and broken keyboards.
